questions.question: What are the two main shared-memory topologies, and where does each win?
options: SQL and NoSQL — SQL always wins

Full message pool (everyone reads everything — simple, inspectable, breaks past ~10 agents as contexts fill) vs blackboard with subscriptions (topic routing — scales to swarms but needs upfront schema design)
RAM and disk — RAM wins under 1GB
Local and global — local always wins
questions.explanation: AutoGen GroupChat and MetaGPT use the pool; CA-MCP and Matrix use subscriptions. Production often mixes: a small full pool at the planning layer, blackboards below at the worker layer.

questions.question: Why is memory poisoning harder to debug than a crash?
options: Logs are encrypted in shared memory
Nothing fails visibly — one agent's hallucinated "fact" gets adopted by every downstream reader, accuracy decays gradually, and the root cause is buried several reasoning steps back
Poisoning only happens in production
It crashes the debugger itself
questions.explanation: In the canonical scenario, agent A misreads "4.2%" as "42%", B summarizes it as fact, C recommends adoption — no agent crashed, no test failed, and the final report cites a number that never existed.

questions.question: Why is memory poisoning STRUCTURAL rather than incidental?
options: It only affects badly-trained models
Without shared state a hallucination stays in one agent's context where downstream re-derivation might catch it; naive shared state launders one agent's context into everyone's as verified fact
It is caused by network packet loss
It only occurs with more than 50 agents
questions.explanation: MAST ranks it the second-most-documented failure family. Any shared-memory design without provenance tracking and an unwritable verifier will exhibit it eventually — the substrate itself does the laundering.

questions.question: Which mitigation set actually works against memory poisoning in production?
options: Larger context windows
Provenance on every entry (who wrote it, from what source), verification before high-stakes reads, and a verifier role that shared state cannot overwrite
Encrypting the shared store
Restarting agents hourly
questions.explanation: Provenance lets a reader distinguish "retrieved from source X" from "asserted by agent A"; verification gates the consequential reads; and keeping the verifier outside the writable surface mirrors the evaluator-firewall rule from the self-improvement lessons.

questions.question: Why does the full message pool stop scaling around ~10 agents?
options: Network bandwidth saturates
Each agent's context window fills with every other agent's work — token cost and context pollution grow with every member, drowning the agent's own task
Message ids overflow
Providers cap concurrent connections at 10
questions.explanation: Transparency has a price: everyone-reads-everything means context grows with the whole team's chatter. Subscription routing exists precisely to spend tokens only on relevant messages.
